speaker: interviewee
text: I'll start from the left. This is my brother, Tsvi, Herman, and this is a bunch of children that were-- the Hebrew school was right across the street from us. And I don't know what class this was, or what they were doing. The girl that is the third one from the right, her name is [? Haya ?] [? Neimann. ?] That's the daughter that her father said, oy, kinder, what did you do? That her sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: The tall one in the background?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number three, yes. He sister was dragged out from her father's house and murdered. And she-- she survived the war and-- and married a cousin of hers that went to Israel-- that was in Israel already in 1929. And I don't know when this picture was taken. It-- it-- it probably was taken maybe in 1929 or '30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the significance of that building to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The building? The building is-- is-- is my house. That's the house that I grew up in. That's our-- our house. It's still in existence now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture-- this girl-- she-- like I said, she married a-- a-- a cousin of hers that lived in Israel. And either they sent it to-- to her, his cousin, or I don't know how. When I was in Israel, I happened to talk to them beause [INAUDIBLE] we always get together. And she told me. She says, I have a picture of your brother. And-- and that's how. And she-- she gave it to me. I-- I made-- made some copies. That's the picture. What the other children are, I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- this picture, on the left, this is my brother Tsvi's wife. How did I get this picture? When I was in the-- in Camp-- in Camp Geretsried, already after the war, in-- in-- this girl that is next to her, she was home-- they come from the same place. And we got to talk, and she said she is from Kolin. I said, Kolin? I had a sister-in-law that lived there. She says, who? I said, Piri. Her name was Piri and her maiden name [? Ala. ?] Oh, she said, and she start pulling out from-- from her purse. She says, I have a little picture. That-- that's my-- that was my girlfriend. And my-- she-- she survived, but my-- my sister-in-law didn't survive. When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: In August?

speaker: interviewee
text: In August. She was already married at that-- in my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. She gave me that picture. That's-- that's why I keep it. It's the only picture I have from-- from-- now, we don't even have-- Moshe had two children. Eliezer had two children. My brother Tsvi had one boy. I have no pictures of those kids at all. I have no pictures of them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK, this-- this picture was taken in 1947. I was 27 years old then. And-- and this was when I was the representative of the IRO, in-- in Camp Geretsried. And this was a-- a camp that kept on revolving. There were people constantly coming in and constantly going out. The people that were emmigrating from this camp, some people were emmigrating to-- to Australia. We have friends that we know-- New Zealand, and South Africa, Canada, and the United States, and Israel, and all over. So that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a document that Mr. Jackson has. It's from the International Refugee Organization, or IRO, US Zone, Germany, dated October 27, 1949. It says, 'subject, work record of Mr. M. Jakubowics. To whom it may concern, Mr. Jakubowics has been employed by UNRRA and the successor agency IRO as camp leader in one of our assembly centers since April 1947 until the present time. Mr. Jakubowics's work during this entire period has been distinguished by energetic application to duty, initiation, thoroughness, and complete reliability. In the course of his work here, Mr. Jakubowics has been exemplary in his behavior, both in work and private life. We recommend Mr. Jakubowics without reservation for any responsible position. Signed, M.--' would you read that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Souget. He was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Area Deputy Director.

speaker: interviewee
text: Area Deputy Director. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is another document of Mr. Jackson's, also from IRO. But this one is dated the 15th of November, 1949, also from US Zone, Germany, Gauting, Area Six, Headquarter Gauting. Subject, your resignation, to Mr. Jakubowics, Camp Leader, Geretsried. Through, Mr. Sorrin, Field Supervisor. 'We received your letter of resignation for which we thank you, but we wish to express our regret at losing so excellent and trusted employee, who has worked for us so long. However, we send our best wishes for your future, and hope you will find in your new country the recognition your excellent qualities deserve. For the Area Director, Matilda Perez De Silva, Chief, Care and Maintenance Department.' [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, This is-- to the left, is Daniel, Jack's oldest son. In the middle is Jack, our son. And to his-- to his left, is Eric. And in front, is Julie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken September 25, '96. That's-- That was his birthday, Jack's birthday. That's it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This one is our daughter-in-law, Cindy. And this is also taken about-- about the same time, I believe. Yeah. And this is our granddaughter, Julie, also in-- it-- it doesn't say on the picture here, but I imagine it was taken because we got them together in the same batch in September '96.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now Mr. Jackson is-- is surrounded by members of his family, and he would like to introduce them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I introduce my dear wife, Nina, and my darling daughter [? Reenee, ?] Renata, I want to say that I regret very much that Jack is three and a half thousand miles away from here and that he can't-- can't be here today. But I-- I hope he'll be here soon, to other happy occasions. And now, I want to say that thanks to-- to Nina, I am here in the United States and not somewhere else. And thanks to my daughter, Renata, I am here at all. Thanks to her insistence that I stop smoking-- that she stopped me about 25 years ago. And I have to say also, thanks to her and my son Jacob, that insisted that I record my experiences of the Shoah years. And now, my darling, Nina, if you want to say a few words.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not many. I think you said everything already that there is to be said about it. And I just join you in saying that we're sorry that Jack and Cindy and the kids are not here but maybe we can repeat a little something in the future. And we're going to say thank you for all the people that worked so hard with Michael and put that all on record, on the tape. And [? Reenee ?]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just wanted also to say thank you to the Shoah Foundation for making it possible for my father to tell his story.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thank you all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks a lot.
